stories.title: June CPI Decelerates from May, Driven by Drop in Energy Index
stories.excerpt: U.S. inflation decelerated in June, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) declining 0.4% after increasing 0.5% in the previous month. A 5.7% drop in the energy index drove the monthly all-items decrease, more than offsetting increases in indexes for shelter and food, the Bureau of Labor Statistics said Tuesday. The energy index was up 15.7% on an annual basis. The monthly decline followed increases of 3.9% in May, 3.8% in April and 10.9% in March. This decline in the all-items index was the largest monthly decrease since April 2020, when it fell 0.8%. On a year-over-year basis, the CPI climbed 3.5%, below the consensus estimate of 3.8% as well as the May annual increase of 4.2%. Core inflation, which excludes food and energy, was unchanged from May, while the Y-O-Y increase of 2.6% came in below a consensus estimate of 2.9%. Indexes that decreased over the month include motor vehicle insurance, communication, apparel, medical care, and used cars and trucks. Conversely, the indexes for recreation, household furnishings and operations, and personal care increased in June. The food index maintained the May monthly increase of 0.2%. The shelter index rose 0.1% in June, below the May level of 0.3%. Both the CPI release and the Producer Price Index announcement on Wednesday occur ahead of Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh’s appearances before the House and Senate. Warsh is scheduled to appear before the House Financial Services Committee at 10 a.m. EDT on Tuesday, and before the Senate Banking Committee at the same time on Wednesday. The post June CPI Decelerates from May, Driven by Drop in Energy Index appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: June CPI fell 0.4% month-over-month, largest drop since April 2020
Year-over-year inflation at 3.5%, below 3.8% consensus; core inflation 2.6%
Energy index down 5.7% monthly; shelter index rose only 0.1%
stories.summary: U.S. inflation decelerated sharply in June as energy prices fell 5.7% month-over-month, driving the all-items CPI down 0.4%—the largest monthly decline since April 2020. Year-over-year CPI came in at 3.5%, beating the 3.8% consensus, while core inflation held at 2.6%. Shelter costs, critical to multifamily underwriting, rose just 0.1% in June versus 0.3% in May, signaling moderating rent pressures.
stories.impact: Slower inflation trajectory reduces Fed rate-cut resistance and improves refinancing visibility for multifamily lenders and sponsors. Deceleration in shelter growth—the metric closest to rent appreciation—suggests rental demand may be normalizing after years of rapid growth; developers should reassess pro-forma rent growth assumptions and cap rate expansion risk across all major markets.

stories.title: RealPage Acquires Cherre in Major Proptech Deal
stories.excerpt: Multifamily software and data analytics giant RealPage announced Tuesday that it has acquired institutional portfolio intelligence firm Cherre. Richardson, Texas-based RealPage acquired Cherre in a cash and money deal, said Dirk Wakeham, president and CEO of RealPage. Wakeham declined to provide specific figures on the acquisition. Manhattan-based Cherre will retain its brand as part of RealPage, said L.D. Salmanson, co-founder and now the former CEO of Cherre. Salmanson will remain with Cherre as the company’s senior vice president, he said. “We are not going away — in fact, the very opposite,” Salmanson said of Cherre, a data platform that connects decision-makers to property investment, management and market information. “We will continue to grow the RealPage ecosystem and continue to grow with our clients. I’m very excited about that. My role essentially is the same, which is to help insurance companies in the world have access to governed trusted data to be able to power their agentic future. And now we have an extra continued mission together, which is to bring that across the entire capital stack, the entire ecosystem, and leverage the existing RealPage product line, which is very broad.” Founded in 2016, Cherre’s platform involves unifying and analyzing more than 4 billion entities and $4 trillion in real assets globally to create a vetted and compliant environment for institutional owners and investors. In acquiring Cherre, RealPage customers will gain access to a layer of intelligence built specifically to bridge property-level data with portfolio- and fund-level context, according to the announcement. “This was more about the value that this brings to our data,” said Wakeham. “We’ve got 25 petabytes [a million gigabytes] of data, and the opportunity for the 1 plus 1 equals 3 here is pointing the Cherre technology, their unified data models and data, against our 25 petabytes of data that’s largely unorganized and somewhat unusable.” In be
bullets: RealPage acquires Manhattan-based Cherre in undisclosed cash deal
Cherre integrates into RealPage ecosystem; retains brand and leadership
Combines multifamily software/analytics (RealPage) with institutional portfolio intelligence (Cherre)
stories.summary: RealPage, the dominant multifamily software and data platform, has acquired Cherre, a Manhattan-based institutional portfolio intelligence firm that provides property investment, management, and market data to institutional investors and insurers. Cherre's founders and leadership remain with the company under RealPage ownership. The deal amount was not disclosed.
stories.impact: Consolidation strengthens RealPage's data moat and analytical depth, giving institutional multifamily owners and developers (RealPage's core customer base) richer market intelligence and portfolio benchmarking. For smaller developers, this reinforces RealPage's dominance and raises switching costs; for large operators, it improves underwriting and asset management tools but may increase software spending. Watch for data-driven market timing advantages flowing to well-capitalized sponsors.

stories.title: Noda Launches Agentic AI for Commercial Building Operations
stories.excerpt: Noda, a leading artificial intelligence startup for commercial building operations, has launched what it calls the first agentic AI providing 24/7 automation to help ameliorate the industry-wide shortage of facility operators. The Washington, D.C.-based company rebranded to its present form in 2023, with the goal of optimizing building operations by mapping data points, automating tasks and driving actionable insights, said Kate Henningsen, CEO of Noda. “This technology was only about 32 percent accurate a year ago,” said Henningsen. “Although people are doing AI in buildings, we have not run across anybody who’s tackling the workflows of building engineers on site. I think it was probably impossible to do this over a year or 18 months ago, and we haven’t seen anybody who was aiding the actions of building operators and engineers in this way.” That created a business opportunity. “Every building in a portfolio is leaving net operating income (NOI) on the table right now, because no team has the capacity to find and fix operational waste at the speed and scale the problem demands,” said Henningsen. “Noda changes that equation. Our AI runs alongside the team you already have, doing the work of a full engineering organization across every asset, every hour of every day — and the impact shows up directly on the profit and loss statement.” Noda’s agentic AI platform creates a fundamental shift in how owners and operators run their buildings and portfolios, the company claims. Unlike analytics tools that surface problems for staff to investigate, Noda’s platform finds, fixes and verifies the problem and its financial impact across every asset, without requiring human intervention. The company has a portfolio of 1,200 buildings, totaling 350 million square feet, based on Salesforce data, said Henningsen. “We’re doing a lot of the building engineering and facilities management operations with new tools,” she said. “There’s great agentic ways to
bullets: Noda launches agentic AI automating 24/7 commercial building operations
Technology improved from 32% to significantly higher accuracy over one year
Addresses industry-wide facility operator shortage by automating building engineer workflows
stories.summary: Noda, a Washington D.C.-based AI startup, has launched agentic AI technology that automates building operations and facility engineer tasks 24/7, addressing a widespread shortage of skilled operators. The company claims it has dramatically improved accuracy from 32% a year ago and is the first to autonomously handle building engineer workflows at scale. The tech maps data, automates tasks, and recovers NOI left on the table.
stories.impact: Reduces labor cost risk and improves NOI recovery on multifamily assets, particularly relevant for larger portfolios where operational inefficiency is measurable. Early adoption by institutional owners will lower per-unit operating expenses and improve competitiveness; widespread deployment could pressure facility operator wages and reduce operator shortage risk—a critical constraint on new supply delivery in tight labor markets.

stories.title: RXR, Waterfall, NYLife Finance $103M Recap of Englewood Cliffs Medical Campus
stories.excerpt: Scott Rechler’s RXR continues to make moves. RXR, Waterfall Asset Management and New York Life Investment Management will finance a $103 million recapitalization of Sylvan Corporate Center, a four-building, 276,000-square-foot medical office campus owned by the Andalex Group in Englewood Cliffs, N.J., Commercial Observer can first report. The new capital will refinance an existing mortgage and support a major expansion of the center’s existing footprint for anchor tenant Englewood Health, which has signed a 20-year lease extension and plans to double its footprint to 202,000 square feet, according to a release. The asset, which opened in 1998, is currently 100 percent leased. “This recapitalization, together with Englewood Health’s long-term expansion, validates the strength of the asset, the quality of the location and the long-term vision we have had for the property,” said Andrew Silverman, principal of Andalex, whose firm acquired Sylvan Corporate Center in 2004 and oversaw the construction of two additional buildings on-site by 2008. Englewood Health currently operates several buildings at Sylvan Corporate Center: an orthopedics and primary care offices, an Asian health and wellness center, and an ambulatory surgery center. The firm is expected to develop a 50,000-square-foot outpatient imaging and therapeutics center and a 50,000-square-foot physician office space as part of the new expansion. “Sylvan Corporate Center represents the type of high-quality, relationship-driven credit investment that RXR is well positioned to execute,” said Russ Young, head of investments at RXR. Englewood Health’s credit profile is also of note, as the provider is scheduled to merge with RWJ Barnabas Health, in a deal that would create the largest combined health care system in the state of New Jersey. Alexander Silverman, another principal at Andalex, called Englewood Health “an exceptional institution and an ideal long-term partner,” in a statement. “Their commitment to more th
bullets: RXR, Waterfall, NYLife Finance $103M recap of Englewood Cliffs medical campus
Anchor tenant Englewood Health signs 20-year extension, doubles footprint to 202K sf
Englewood Cliffs, N.J. asset currently 100% leased; expansion drives refinance
stories.summary: RXR, Waterfall Asset Management, and New York Life Investment Management are refinancing a $103 million recapitalization of Sylvan Corporate Center, a 276,000-sf medical office campus in Englewood Cliffs, N.J., owned by Andalex Group. Anchor tenant Englewood Health has signed a 20-year lease extension and plans to expand to 202,000 sf from its current footprint. The asset is fully leased and the expansion is driving new capital.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. However, signals strong healthcare real estate fundamentals in the NYC suburbs and demonstrates healthy long-term lease growth; medical office strength can indirectly support multifamily demand in secondary markets (Englewood Cliffs) via employment stability and professional-class retention.

stories.title: Gaia CEO Danny Fishman Grew the Development Firm by Looking Way Ahead
stories.excerpt: Danny Fishman swims against the current. Unlike most companies during the Global Financial Crisis (GFC) — which from 2007 to 2009 triggered a worldwide economic downturn — Fishman, co-founder and CEO of GAIA Real Estate, took the opportunity to be extremely active in deal-making. Not only did he launch Gaia during the period, but he also hit his professional stride by acquiring distressed properties — properties no other company would dare buy during a financial crisis — for steep discounts, flipping them into desirable assets, and selling them for more than triple what they’re worth. “Danny’s been very successful in not falling where a lot of other people fell,” said Doron Broman, founder and CEO of Moderno Development Group and a frequent business partner of Gaia. “His strategy is very smart, and he’s very disciplined.” Fishman’s tactic of acquiring mispriced assets has turned Gaia, a private equity and property development firm focusing on acquiring, renovating and managing residential properties across the U.S., into a prominent institutional investor. All told, the firm — along with its privately held real estate investment trust Gaia REIT — has a portfolio encompassing more than 80 properties, 20,000 residential units and $4 billion in assets under management. But all of that success came after Fishman grew the firm’s humble roots. Fishman was born and raised in Jerusalem, launching an early career preparing large Israeli government-owned real estate companies for privatization. He then moved on to found Tamir Fishman, an Israeli investment bank and asset management firm that’s still growing today. Fishman eventually met his business partner and Gaia co-founder, Ken Woolley, in Israel around 2008. From there, Gaia was born, taking its name from Greek mythology. (Gaia is the Earth.) The co-founders hit it off and both saw what they called a “rare opportunity” at the time, which was to invest in apartments during what was going to be an epic downturn during the
bullets: GAIA Real Estate CEO Danny Fishman built firm by acquiring distressed properties during GFC
Strategy: buy mispriced assets during crises, renovate, sell at multiples of acquisition cost
GAIA specializes in residential acquisition, renovation, and management across U.S.
stories.summary: Danny Fishman, co-founder and CEO of GAIA Real Estate, built a prominent residential development and investment firm by taking contrarian risk during the 2007–2009 financial crisis, acquiring distressed properties at steep discounts and flipping them for multiples of cost. GAIA focuses on acquiring, renovating, and managing residential properties nationwide and has become a major institutional investor through disciplined underwriting and opportunistic timing.
stories.impact: Illustrates the enduring playbook for multifamily value creation: buy mispriced assets when capital is scarce, control basis, and sell or hold as refinancing becomes available. Signals importance of capital discipline and dry powder positioning during macro uncertainty; developers with patient capital and strong underwriting will have outsized returns in the next dislocation cycle.

stories.title: Information Is Becoming Cheaper. Judgment Is Becoming Priceless.
stories.excerpt: For most of my career, one of the greatest competitive advantages you could possess was information. If you knew more than your competitors, you had an edge. You knew who owned what, who was refinancing, who was thinking about selling, what buyers were paying, where rents were moving, and what was happening before everyone else did. That information allowed you to make better decisions and create opportunities others simply couldn’t see. In many respects, that informational advantage was one of the foundations upon which my old firm Massey Knakal was built. We invested enormous amounts of time collecting, organizing and studying market information. We knew more about New York City investment properties than anyone else because we made it our business to know everything about something, as opposed to knowing something about everything. That knowledge became an enormous competitive advantage and ultimately helped us build what became the most dominant investment sales company in the history of the United States. Robert Knakal. PHOTO: Patrick McMullan/Patrick McMullan via Getty Images But the world is changing. Artificial intelligence is rapidly commoditizing information. Today, virtually anyone can ask an AI platform a sophisticated question and receive an intelligent, comprehensive answer in seconds. Market statistics, legal precedents, financial models, zoning information, demographic trends and research that once required years to accumulate are now available almost instantly. The cost of acquiring information is collapsing. Economics teaches us that when something becomes abundant, its value inevitably declines. That doesn’t mean expertise is becoming less valuable. Quite the opposite. It means the source of expertise is changing. As information becomes commoditized, judgment becomes the new competitive advantage. Judgment is the ability to look at the same information everyone else has and arrive at a better conclusion. It is knowing which facts actua
bullets: AI commoditizes market data; information advantage eroding for RE professionals
Judgment and deal sourcing becoming new competitive edge over raw market intel
Author notes shift from Massey Knakal's data-hoarding to AI-democratized landscape
stories.summary: As AI rapidly commoditizes access to real estate market information, the traditional competitive advantage of information arbitrage is disappearing. Market data, deal sourcing intel, and pricing trends are now available to anyone via AI; the new edge lies in judgment, relationships, deal sourcing, and speed of execution rather than privileged data. The author, a founder of Massey Knakal, contrasts the old information-dominant model with today's leveled playing field.
stories.impact: Accelerates consolidation of deal-sourcing and underwriting advantage toward firms with strong broker networks, institutional relationships, and capital—not data. Smaller and undercapitalized development firms will struggle to compete on deal flow and timing; larger platforms with multiple capital sources and broad GP networks will command the market. Forces developers to compete on execution, cost control, and sponsor reputation rather than information edge.

stories.title: Buyer scores $2M judgment against Mandarin Oriental Boca developer
stories.excerpt: The legal troubles at Penn-Florida’s Mandarin Oriental Boca Raton continue to mount after the developer lost a $2 million judgment.  A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge sided with Douglas and Debra Jacobs after they filed a lawsuit against Penn-Florida affiliate Via Mizner Owner III, which alleged the developer failed to complete their condo in time, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  The developer announced plans for the 248-unit hotel-condo Mandarin Oriental in 2015, with an initial opening target of 2017, the outlet reported. The complaint alleged that the Jacobses signed a contract to buy a condo in the development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Penn-Florida's Via Mizner loses $2M judgment in Palm Beach County for condo delay
Jacobses signed 2022 contract with Dec. 31, 2025 completion date; unit not finished
13 total lawsuits filed against Via Mizner over Mandarin Oriental delays; one other settled for $2.1M
stories.summary: A Palm Beach County Circuit Court judge ruled against Penn-Florida's Via Mizner Owner III affiliate, awarding the Jacobses a $2 million judgment for failure to complete their condo unit by the December 31, 2025 deadline. The couple had signed a purchase agreement in 2022 and will receive refund authorization from escrow. Via Mizner faces 13 lawsuits over Mandarin Oriental delivery delays; another buyer recently settled for $2.1 million.
stories.impact: South Florida condo developer liability risk rising sharply due to completion delays and contract breaches. Signals demand softness or cost overruns forcing delays on speculative condo projects; buyers are holding developers accountable in court and winning. For multifamily developers, illustrates risk of over-leveraging spec condo pipelines tied to market timing and the importance of contingency reserves for cost inflation and labor constraints.

stories.title: Chicago proposes $287M TIF raid for Related Midwest’s megaproject The 78
stories.excerpt: Chicago officials are set to throw $287 million of property tax revenue into the megaproject that includes Major League Soccer team the Chicago Fire’s new stadium in the South Loop.  The proposal for the project known as the 78 is being considered by the Chicago City Council Finance Committee. The shift in funding is expected to partially reorganize a tax increment financing subsidy for the project’s developer, Related Midwest, according to Crain’s.  The revised agreement would add $287 million from the Canal/Congress TIF district to the TIF district that the 78 sits in, meaning the project’s developers would not have […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Chicago proposes $287M TIF raid on Canal/Congress district to fund 'The 78' megaproject
The 78 includes Chicago Fire MLS stadium and mixed-use development by Related Midwest
Shift redirects Union Station maintenance funds; would capture ~9 years of Canal/Congress TIF revenue
stories.summary: Chicago city officials propose redirecting $287 million in property tax increment financing (TIF) revenue from the Canal/Congress district to fund infrastructure for The 78, a megaproject anchored by the Chicago Fire's new MLS stadium in the South Loop developed by Related Midwest. The move is controversial because it reallocates funds earmarked for Union Station maintenance and would essentially consume all Canal/Congress TIF revenue for nine years. Original plan was only $85 million over three years.
stories.impact: TIF raid signals Chicago's commitment to South Loop placemaking and large mixed-use development but creates fiscal drag on competing infrastructure projects (Union Station). Increased public subsidy reduces equity returns for private developers on large urban renewal projects; developers bidding on Chicago catalytic projects should model optimistic TIF participation but lower sponsor IRR assumptions. Success of The 78 as a mixed-use anchor could drive multifamily demand in South Loop but at reduced unit economics due to land cost escalation and longer entitlement timelines.

stories.title: Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center
stories.excerpt: Glasshouse has signed a long-term lease for 66,436 square feet at 3 World Trade Center. The 30-year agreement for a portion of the ground floor and the entirety of the second and third floors brings the event, hospitality and experiential venue operator to the World Trade Center campus for its fourth Manhattan location. CBRE’s Chris Mansfield, Anthony Dattoma and Zachary Weil represented Glasshouse. The transaction involved a three-party structure among Glasshouse, Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (URW) and the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. URW represented itself. “This transaction represents one of the most significant experiential leasing transactions completed in Manhattan in recent years,” said Dattoma. “Beyond creating a flagship venue for Glasshouse, this lease complements the continued momentum and surge of leasing activity across Lower Manhattan. The new venue is expected to attract more than 250,000 visitors annually, bringing additional energy and activation to the World Trade Center campus while further strengthening Downtown as one of New York City’s premier destinations for business, hospitality, culture and community engagement.” The post Events Operator Glasshouse Signs 30-Year Lease for 3 World Trade Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Glasshouse events operator signs 30-year lease for 66,436 sf at 3 World Trade Center
Three-party structure: Glasshouse, URW, Port Authority of NY/NJ
Venue expected to attract 250K+ visitors annually; fourth Manhattan Glasshouse location
stories.summary: Glasshouse, an event and experiential venue operator, has signed a 30-year lease for 66,436 square feet across ground, second, and third floors of 3 World Trade Center in Lower Manhattan. The transaction is structured among Glasshouse, developer Unibail-Rodamco-Westfield (URW), and the Port Authority. The venue is projected to draw over 250,000 visitors annually and is Glasshouse's fourth Manhattan location.
stories.impact: Long-term activation and foot traffic anchor at WTC campus improves neighborhood vitality and residential appeal; signals sustained institutional confidence in Lower Manhattan as a mixed-use destination. For multifamily developers, validates continued demand for residential stock near transit-rich, culturally activated downtown nodes; supports rents and absorption in nearby Battery Park City, South Street Seaport, and downtown Brooklyn residential projects.

stories.title: Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement
stories.excerpt: Northmarq’s Los Angeles Investment Sales team of Mike Hanassab, Steven Goldstein and Elliot Hassan arranged the $5.25-million sale of Hyperion Residences, a three-parcel development site located at 835 N. Hyperion Ave. in Los Angeles’ Silver Lake neighborhood. Northmarq represented the seller, Hyperion Apartments LP. The buyer is a locally based affordable housing developer. The site was delivered fully entitled and Ready-to-Issue for a six-story, approximately 54-unit project under Transit Oriented Communities Tier 3. During escrow, the buyer leveraged Executive Directive 1 to re-entitle the parcels for 105 units, increasing density and reducing per-unit cost significantly. The community is now under construction as a mixed-income development. “Silver Lake remains one of Los Angeles’ most entitlement-constrained corridors, and sites that pencil for Executive Directive 1 projects are increasingly sought after,” said Hanassab. “The buyer recognized the deeper opportunity and moved swiftly to re-entitle the parcels during escrow, bringing their basis to roughly $50,000 per buildable unit — an exceptional outcome for both parties.” Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Silver Lake Development Parcel Increases Density Through Re-Entitlement appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hyperion Residences (Silver Lake, Los Angeles) sold for $5.25M fully entitled for 54 units
Buyer re-entitled during escrow via Executive Directive 1 to 105 units (+94% density)
Final basis ~$50K per buildable unit; mixed-income project now under construction
stories.summary: Northmarq arranged the $5.25 million sale of Hyperion Residences, a three-parcel development site in Silver Lake, Los Angeles, delivered fully entitled for a 54-unit Transit Oriented Communities project. The buyer, a local affordable housing developer, leveraged Executive Directive 1 during escrow to re-entitle the parcels for 105 units, a 94% density increase. Final basis achieved ~$50,000 per buildable unit—an exceptional outcome reflecting the buyer's entitlement arbitrage.
stories.impact: Los Angeles' Executive Directive 1 (streamlined approval for affordable projects) is creating significant value capture for agile developers with entitlement expertise; Silver Lake remains highly constrained, making density arbitrage critical to project feasibility. Multifamily developers should prioritize ED1 entitlement strategies and re-entitlement optionality in acquisition underwriting; $50K/unit basis in Silver Lake is highly competitive and signals strong builder demand for entitled sites. Trend favors institutional sponsors with in-house entitlement teams.

stories.title: Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside
stories.excerpt: Construction has begun at 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., a 19-story condominium rising at the border of Sunnyside. The development is led by Hiwin Group USA, whose previous work includes 300 W. 30th St. in Chelsea, a 16-story, 69-residence condo project that sold out in nine months. Scheduled for delivery in 2029, 51-02 Roosevelt Ave. represents a rare high-rise addition to the neighborhood, according to Acre NY Realty, which is serving as sales and marketing representative.”51-02 Roosevelt Ave. is more than a new residential building. It is a meaningful addition to the Sunnyside community,” said Cathy Huang, CEO of Acre NY Realty. “With its height, visibility program and strong transit access, this project has the potential to become a true neighborhood landmark.” The project will include 84 residential units, including 70 one-bedroom and 14 two-bedroom residences. It will also provide ground-floor retail, commercial office space, community-oriented medical space, residential recreation areas, terraces, and parking access. The post Condo High-Rise Launches Construction in Sunnyside appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Hiwin Group USA begins construction on 51-02 Roosevelt Ave., Sunnyside, Queens
19-story condo tower, 84 units, delivery 2029; 70 one-bed, 14 two-bed units
Rare high-rise in neighborhood; developer behind 69-unit Chelsea project (sold out in 9 months)
stories.summary: Construction has launched at 51-02 Roosevelt Ave. in Sunnyside, Queens, a 19-story condominium developed by Hiwin Group USA scheduled for delivery in 2029. The project includes 84 units (70 one-bedroom, 14 two-bedroom) with ground-floor retail, office, medical space, and parking. Hiwin previously developed a 69-unit Chelsea condo that sold out in nine months, demonstrating strong execution track record.
stories.impact: Signals continued condo investor confidence in Queens secondary markets near transit. While rental multifamily focused, the project's scale and rapid pre-sales (Chelsea precedent) validate demand for housing near Sunnyside Transit Hub; rental developers should monitor condo absorption rates as a demand barometer for the submarket. Long 2029 delivery timeline suggests current entitlements and construction financing remain available in outer boroughs despite broader NYC market softness.

stories.title: Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of an industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Rd. in Middleton, MA. The asset sold for $2,525,000. “The quality of this industrial condo drove interest from owner-users and investors,” said Mattias Edenkrans, associate director investments in Marcus & Millichap’s Boston office. “Ultimately, the low market vacancy in this core North Shore market appealed to an investor group that purchased with conviction, allowing us to achieve certainty of execution for our client.” Edenkrans and Luigi Lessa of Marcus & Millichap marketed the property on behalf of the seller, E Homes Realty, LLC, and procured the buyer, Richardson Green. The property, located at 30 Log Bridge Road #303, is a 12,975-square-foot industrial condominium on a 13.51-acre lot. The property is split into a 7,025-square-foot warehouse, a 2,975-square-foot showroom on the first floor and a 2,975-square-foot second-floor office space. It was built in 2016 and includes 25 parking spaces. The post Marcus & Millichap Closes Sale of North Shore Industrial Condo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Marcus & Millichap closed sale of 12,975-sf industrial condo in Middleton, MA for $2.525M
Property sold to investor group (Richardson Green) citing low North Shore market vacancy
Built 2016; includes warehouse, showroom, office, 25 parking spaces on 13.51-acre lot
stories.summary: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of an industrial condominium at 30 Log Bridge Road in Middleton, Massachusetts for $2.525 million. The 12,975-sf property, built in 2016, includes warehouse, showroom, and office space on a 13.51-acre parcel. The buyer was an investor group attracted by tight North Shore industrial vacancy and asset quality.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily. North Shore Boston industrial vacancy tightness suggests strong regional logistics/e-commerce demand and employment base that indirectly supports multifamily fundamentals in suburban Boston; however, article lacks direct multifamily signal.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas –– but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Judge dismisses Soho Development suit vs. Two Roads over $300M Four Seasons Bahamas condo project
Two Roads and Access Industries (Len Blavatnik) developing 67-unit Ocean Club; construction to 2028
Soho alleges breach of 2021 NDA and sidelining from project; plaintiff says judge dismissed wrong complaint
stories.summary: A judge dismissed Soho Development's lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining the firm from a ~$300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas, though Soho claims the judge addressed an outdated complaint filing. Two Roads and Access Industries are developing Ocean Club, Four Seasons Residences (67 units, Paradise Island) with presales underway and 2028 completion target. Soho alleges breach of a 2021 NDA and non-circumvention clause.
stories.impact: Illustrates partner/sponsor disputes in large luxury condo developments and litigation risk in joint ventures; relevant to multifamily sponsors considering JV structures with institutional capital partners. South Florida luxury condo market remains litigious and capital-constrained post-pandemic, signaling slower absorption and risk of holdups; multifamily developers should avoid speculative condo partnerships and focus on institutional rental multifamily where demand is clearer.

stories.title: Blake Poston jumps from the gridiron to the high-rise with plans for a $100M Uptown Dallas apartment project
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Blake Poston (former SMU football player) developing $100M, 20-story high-rise in Uptown Dallas
The Savannah: 100-unit luxury rental building, avg. 1,800 sf units, 3 penthouse units
Cover2 Capital (Poston's firm) also developing Wheelhouse (297 units, North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (Anna)
stories.summary: Blake Poston, founder of Cover2 Capital and former SMU football player, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment tower at 5220 McKinney Avenue in Uptown Dallas with 100 units (mostly 1–3 bedrooms) plus three penthouses. Construction is targeted to begin late 2025. Cover2 is also behind Wheelhouse (297 units, North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse (Anna), demonstrating depth in Dallas-area multifamily development.
stories.impact: New luxury supply entering tight Uptown Dallas submarket; 100-unit boutique positioning (1,800-sf avg units) suggests targeting high-rent demographic and premium rents. Larger competitive projects (300+ units) from established sponsors will pressure occupancy and rent growth, but demographic fit (young professionals, affluent singles/couples) remains strong in Uptown. Monitor rents at similar competitive properties (nearby luxury towers) to gauge early lease-up momentum and whether market absorbs 100+ units of new luxury supply without significant rate compression.

stories.title: South Florida Dirt: A timeline of the Vacchi vs. Stern legal battle
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Stern teamed up with Italian investor and TikToker Gianluca Vacchi in 2024.  At the time, Stern said that Vacchi “understands new media and how to cut through the noise on social media in a way most real estate people don’t,” calling him an effective marketer and tastemaker. Online to his 22.1 million Instagram followers and 22.3 million TikTok followers, Vacchi has showcased his jetsetting lifestyle, workouts, cold plunges, and maybe most frequently, his dancing.  Two years ago, Stern and Vacchi said they were 50-50 partners on a $4 billion pipeline of JDS Development’s projects (Stern’s firm). That included […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: JDS Development (Michael Stern) partnership with Gianluca Vacchi (social media influencer) dissolving
Vacchi sues Stern alleging Ponzi scheme, fund diversion between $4B project pipeline
Affected projects: Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, 888 Brickell, 1250 West Avenue, Casablanca Miami Beach
stories.summary: Developer Michael Stern's partnership with Italian investor and social media influencer Gianluca Vacchi (22M Instagram, 22M TikTok followers) has imploded. Vacchi has sued Stern alleging a Ponzi scheme and misrepresentation of project status, claiming Stern diverted his capital between projects in the $4 billion JDS Development pipeline. Litigation has stalled high-profile Miami condo projects including Mercedes-Benz Places, 888 Brickell, and 1250 West Avenue.
stories.impact: South Florida luxury condo market turmoil continues; high-profile projects tied up in litigation with institutional equity exiting. JDS has secured new debt and partnered with Jeff Soffer for Mercedes-Benz Places rescue, signaling distress. Multifamily developers should avoid spec condo partnerships with influencer/celebrity equity, as governance and capital discipline are poor substitutes for institutional oversight. Pipeline delays and litigation signal weakening South Florida presales velocity; multifamily fundamentals (rental demand, NOI stability) remain stronger than condo sales.

stories.title: Mark Cuban takes Adelson to court on Mavs plans for new arena
stories.excerpt: Who could’ve guessed that selling a basketball team would change who’s in charge? Apparently not Mark Cuban.  After hinting at his regret over selling the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands on a podcast in March, the billionaire businessman isn’t just going public with his concerns about the team’s new ownership–he’s taking them to court.  Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the team to Miriam Adelson’s casino company in 2023, claims in a new court filing that he was shut out of decisions about the team’s new arena, the Dallas Morning News reported. He’s asking the court to grant […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mark Cuban sues Adelson's Las Vegas Sands over Dallas Mavericks arena deal at Valley View Mall
Cuban claims verbal agreement excluded him from new arena decisions after 2023 Sands sale
Texas casino gambling legalization still blocked despite lobbying; arena contingent on passage
stories.summary: Mark Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands owner Miriam Adelson in 2023, is suing to assert review rights over the team's planned new arena at the Valley View Mall site. Cuban claims a verbal agreement with Sands preserved his operational oversight, but Sands has excluded him from key decisions. The arena development is contingent on Texas legalizing casino gambling, which has stalled despite years of lobbying and spending.
stories.impact: Mavericks arena/casino project likely delayed due to litigation and legislative uncertainty around gaming legalization. Uptown Dallas multifamily supply pipeline linked to Valley View redevelopment may face uncertainty; developers should deprioritize near-term absorption of large Valley View-adjacent supply and monitor arena litigation closure before major commitments. Texas casino legalization failure removes major economic catalyst for Dallas downtown activation; multifamily demand remains strong but mega-project uncertainty should not anchor development timing.
